analysis.note: Privative a-/nañ form; taken as a masculine nominative predicate agreeing with asau.

verification.detail: compound stem acitta as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Privative an-/nañ form: ‘non-apprehension/non-perception’; masculine nominative predicate with asau. Corrected stem is anupalamba, matching the bhāṣya reading anupalambo; anupalambha would derive anupalambhaḥ/anupalambho, not the printed form.

verification.detail: compound stem anupalamba as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Nom. sg. masculine demonstrative; its masculine gender groups it with acittaḥ and anupalambaḥ rather than with neuter jñānam.
Neuter nominative singular predicate with tat; formally also accusative, but nominative is selected by the predicative construction.
Adjective agreeing with jñānam/tat; bhāṣya sandhi has final -m assimilated before ca as ñ.

verification.detail: compound stem lokottara as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Neuter nominative singular demonstrative, agreeing with jñānam lokottaram.
Genitive dependent on parāvṛttiḥ: ‘turning/transformation of the basis/support’.
Feminine nominative singular; final visarga appears as -r before dvidhā.
Indeclinable ‘in two ways, twofold’; grammar alone leaves its exact scope between hāni and dauṣṭhulya somewhat open.
Ablatival/causal indeclinable in -taḥ, understood from the compound stem dauṣṭhulyahāni: ‘because of the abandonment/removal of badness’.

verification.detail: avyayībhāva; whole form not in kosha/MW as indeclinable

justifications.decision: The gender mismatch is best handled by grouping the three masculine forms together and the three neuter forms together, rather than forcing acittaḥ/anupalambaḥ to agree with jñānam.
options_considered: All forms predicate directly of neuter jñānam
Masculine acittaḥ/anupalambaḥ/asau form one predication, neuter jñānam/lokottaram/tat another
Take asau adverbially, against the supplied analysis
justifications.chosen: acittaḥ anupalambaḥ asau forms a masculine nominative predication, while jñānam lokottaram ca tat forms a neuter nominative predication.

evidence.translation: The printed sequence contains masculine nominative singular acittaḥ, anupalambaḥ, asau, followed by neuter nominative singular jñānam, lokottaram, tat.

justifications.decision: The neuter nominative singular adjective lokottaram agrees with neuter nominative singular jñānam and tat. The dictionary sense ‘surpassing the world, extraordinary’ supports ‘supramundane’ in a Buddhist philosophical verse.
options_considered: Take lokottaram as an adjective agreeing with jñānam/tat
Render merely as ‘extraordinary’ without the loka- ‘world’ force

evidence.translation: jñānam, lokottaram, and tat are all neuter singular forms, naturally read in apposition/predication.

justifications.decision: The genitive āśrayasya is construed dependently on the nominative noun parāvṛttiḥ. Nothing in the grammar requires an ablatival ‘from the basis’; the given case is genitive.
justifications.chosen: āśrayasya parāvṛttiḥ means ‘the turning/transformation of the basis’.

evidence.translation: ‘parāvṛttiḥ of āśraya’: a genitive relation between ‘basis/support’ and ‘turning/transformation’.

justifications.decision: The ending -taḥ is not the normal ablative singular of the feminine i-stem hāni; therefore the form is better understood as an indeclinable -taḥ formation from the compound dauṣṭhulya-hāni. dvidhā is translated broadly as ‘twofold/in two ways’.
options_considered: hānitaḥ as a regular feminine ablative singular of hāni
hānitaḥ as a past participle from a verbal root
justifications.chosen: dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ is a causal/ablatival indeclinable: ‘because of the abandonment/removal of badness’.

evidence.translation: The sequence is read as dvidhā + dauṣṭhulya-hānitaḥ: ‘twofold / because of abandonment of badness’.

justifications.decision: Grammar alone does not decide whether dvidhā primarily qualifies the abandonment/removal or the badness removed. A compact ‘twofold abandonment of badness’ preserves the ambiguity better than a fully specified doctrinal paraphrase.
options_considered: Because of abandoning badness in two ways
Because of a twofold removal of badness
justifications.chosen: dvidhā is left with a broad ‘twofold’ scope in translation.

evidence.translation: dvidhā is an indeclinable immediately preceding the compound; either ‘in two ways’ or ‘twofold’ is grammatically possible.

translation:
Non-mind (acitta), non-apprehension (anupalambha)—this;
and that is supramundane knowledge (lokottara-jñāna).
It is the turning of the basis (āśraya-parāvṛtti),
because of the twofold abandonment of badness (dauṣṭhulya).
one_shot_delta: With no commentary, the referent of the masculine asau and the reason for the switch to neuter tat/jñānam remain inferential rather than secured by exposition.
A commentary-blind translation may over-specify dvidhā as either ‘two kinds of badness’ or ‘two kinds of abandonment’; the grammar by itself does not force one scope.

A purely dictionary-based rendering of acitta as ‘inconceivable’ or ‘unnoticed’ is possible from MW, but the verse’s pairing with anupalambha and jñāna favors the more technical ‘non-mind/non-mental’ rendering.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 api_seg offers dauṣṭhulya_hānyāḥ, but the supplied bhāṣya line and local_S read dauṣṭhulya_hānitaḥ; I analyze the printed form dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ.

ByT5 local_SLM treats hānitaḥ as hāni_SBF, i.e. a subanta-like feminine ablative analysis. I instead take dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ as an indeclinable -taḥ/tasil causal form, since a regular feminine i-stem ablative singular of hāni would not be hānitaḥ.
ByT5 tokenizes dauṣṭhulya and hānitaḥ separately; I record the syntactic word as the compound-derived indeclinable dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ.
open_questions: The precise antecedent or implied masculine noun for acittaḥ anupalambaḥ asau is not recoverable from the verse grammar alone.
The exact doctrinal content of the ‘twofold’ in dvidhā is not determined without commentary or context.
The technical nuance of āśraya in āśrayasya parāvṛttiḥ is rendered as ‘basis’, but the verse alone does not define it.
